channel.title: Space on rirsc
channel.description: Recent content in Space on rirsc

item.title: 25week16 - Brain texts. Mapping universe. A golden hat
item.description:
This week I finally grabbed a sunny coffee at Cafe Finovo, located at the entrance of a little cemetery. It was sunny and the cherry blossom bloomed, so the experience forced us to grab a second one to elongate the nice time.

˙

I am not discovering anything new here, but the newsletter from Anne-Laure Le Cunff↗ has quite a collection of interesting notes about the brain and human behavior.

˙

Hypertext TV↗ is a ‘real’ TV channel where you can zap around media in the forms of games, music, poetry and more by Ky Decker↗. You can make your boss dance↗ or, you know, wonder about your entire existence and read about the fourth dimension↗. Go check the rest.

˙

The Women Who Mapped the Universe and Still Couldn’t Get Any Respect↗.

˙

Lately I am reading a bit about Sky Atlas and how humans have approached stargazing. I discovered this golden hat↗, which apparently was used as a lunar and solar calendar. Imagining that being worn makes me wonder if its adding serenity or funny to the wizard. Anyhow, its kept at the Neues Museum; I will go see it one of these days.

˙

Listening: Stander - Collapsing because I watched them this weekend:

item.title: Arecibo Message and Poetica Vagina
item.description:
Fifty years ago, the Arecibo Message was sent—a human attempt to contact whatever might be out there, potentially listening in space. It consisted of numbers, formulas, and representations of ourselves, as well as a depiction of the telescope from which it was sent, located in Arecibo.

I love the idea and its aesthetics—I guess because it reminds me of my beloved Game of Life↗ It makes me wonder: would it be possible to recreate that exact drawing from one of the initial configurations?

From there, I did a quick search for various messages humanity has sent into space in hopes of finding life.Voyager Golden Record (1977), Cosmic Call (1999), the Teen Age Message (2008), and perhaps one of the oldest and most famous; the Pioneer Plaques. As many have pointed out, the Pioneer Plaques offered a vague representation of humanity’s cultural and racial diversity. The depiction of the woman’s body notably omits genitalia, making it feel incomplete. While searching for a more accurate or inclusive representation, somehow I ended learning about the project by Joe Davis: Poetica Vagina↗

In the 1980s he came up with a project that records the vaginal contractions of ballerinas using a “vaginal detector” built in a laboratory of mechanical engineering. […] He used electronic music software in order to match the the frequency of the contractions to the English speech. He then transmitted the sounds of vaginal contractions to the nearest star systems.

I’m just not really sure what to think about this project at all. Lets pass to a brief musical break: Vèjula - Merope↗
